summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, Science / computing, Education / pedagogy. The strongest items in this run are JD Vance defends Trump amid spat with Pope Leo: ‘Stick to matters of morality’, Anbernic’s new handheld is a pocket-friendly Android device with a swiveling screen, and Samsung is hiking the prices of its Galaxy phones and tablets.

mainItems.title: Idols Of Ash review - a terrific spelunking horror game that makes you choose between a rock and a giant centipede
mainItems.summary: When I think of Idols of Ash , I think of seven sounds, listed here in order of deepening dreadfulness. The first and nicest sound is the clink of my silvery grapple sinking its hook, interrupting the rush of air past my ears as I fall.

mainItems.title: Opinion: America Has a Million Untapped Tutors. Here’s How to Activate Them
mainItems.summary: There are more than 12 million elementary and middle school students from low-income families who are below grade level in reading or math, our analysis shows.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: AI companies make powerful tech – but they’re also savvy marketers
mainItems.summary: Anthropic’s Claude Mythos AI is said to be frighteningly capable, but we shouldn’t get carried away by the hype Hello, and welcome to TechScape.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Marathon will test out a new experimental game mode which forces players to use free sponsored kits
mainItems.summary: If you've been playing Marathon and have been bodied by geared-out players, packing fully upgraded guns and purple shields, a new experimental game mode coming this week may be exactly what you've been waiting for. Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Has Google’s AI watermarking system been reverse-engineered?
mainItems.summary: A software developer claims to have reverse-engineered Google DeepMind's SynthID system, showing how AI watermarks can be stripped from generated images or manually inserted into other works. A claim that, according to Google, isn't true.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Google, Microsoft, Meta All Tracking You Even When You Opt Out, According to an Independent Audit
mainItems.summary: “This is the Strait of Hormuz in the data economy. If you want to make a change, this is where you cut it off. Anything short of that is theatrical political posture.”

mainItems.title: The glossy, golden frosting you probably haven't made yet: Courtesy of the French.
mainItems.summary: The country of France has provided the baking canon with no shortage of classics. But outside of croissants, éclairs, madeleines, and macarons, there’s still one classic recipe that flies under the radar.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Lower-stakes, but high fit for the maker/craft side of your brain.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.
